title: Electrically switchable vacancy state revealed by in-operando positron experiments
authors: Ric Fulop, Laurence Lyons, Robert Nick, Marc H. Weber, Ming Liu, Haig Atikian, Uwe Bauer, Alexander C. Barbati, Neil Gershenfeld
abstract: Whether the flash state in electrically driven solids involves non-equilibrium defect production or is accounted for by Joule heating alone has been debated since 2010. Using positron annihilation spectroscopy on copper, we observe a fully reversible, electrically switchable vacancy population: the DBS S-parameter rises above baseline whenever applied current exceeds a critical density and returns on current removal. Positron lifetime spectroscopy independently confirms open-volume defect formation and reveals a void to cluster relaxation hierarchy. The current-induced vacancy concentration exceeds the thermal-equilibrium value at 352C by > 106x, is present only while current is applied, and vanishes within minutes. The nucleation rate scales steeply with the applied current, connecting the minute-scale kinetics resolved here to the sub-second flash events observed in ceramic sintering. These results demonstrate current-induced Frenkel-pair production in a metal and identify a defect-mediated, non-equilibrium contribution to the flash state.
summary: This paper investigates the origin of the 'flash' state in metals, a phenomenon where high currents cause rapid changes in conductivity. Using in-situ positron spectroscopy on copper, the researchers discovered that applying current above a critical threshold creates a transient, reversible population of vacancies. This vacancy concentration is much higher than what would be expected from thermal heating alone, suggesting a non-equilibrium, current-driven mechanism. The findings provide a new way to understand defect dynamics in materials under extreme electrical stress.
main_problem: Determining whether the 'flash' phenomenon in electrically driven solids is caused by purely thermal Joule heating or a non-equilibrium, current-induced defect production mechanism.
main_result: The study reveals a fully reversible, electrically switchable vacancy population in copper that exceeds thermal equilibrium concentrations by over six orders of magnitude, supporting a non-equilibrium, field-driven defect production model.
method: In-operando positron annihilation spectroscopy, including Doppler Broadening Spectroscopy (DBS) and Positron Annihilation Lifetime Spectroscopy (PALS), combined with four-point resistivity and multi-method thermometry.
model_or_system: The experimental system consists of electrolytic copper (Cu) foils subjected to high electrical current densities, specifically investigating the regime near a critical current density threshold.
key_observables: S-parameter (Doppler Broadening), W-parameter, positron lifetime (tau), electrical resistivity, and temperature.
important_parameters: Critical current density (Jc approx 110 A/mm2), vacancy concentration (130-530 ppm), and a rate-scaling exponent (alpha approx 20).
main_assumptions: The saturation value of the defect S-parameter (Sd) is taken from reported cluster/void contrasts rather than being measured directly.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates positron trapping and depth profiles; Figure 2 shows S-parameter and current-induced regimes (annealing vs. switching); Figure 3 shows resistivity deviations; Figure 5 shows S-parameter increases at high current.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the flash state debate, describes the experimental positron spectroscopy setup, presents the identification of reversible vacancy switching, details the kinetic scaling and defect hierarchy, and concludes by ruling out purely thermal origins.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Positron DBS/PALS technique and experimental setup. a, Positron trapping con- cept. In a defect-free copper lattice the thermalised positron (orange cloud) is delocalised over a Bloch state with a thermal de Broglie wavelength of several nanometres (thousands of atomic sites; the schematic shows only a few unit cells) and annihilates predominantly with valence electrons, yield- ing a low-S, high-W signature. At an open-volume defect (right; dashed circle marks the missing atom) the positron is localised in the vacancy and samples a different electron-momentum distribu- tion, yielding a high-S, low-W signature. b, The S and W windows on the 511 keV annihilation photopeak. The...

Fig. 2 Reversible vacancy switching under current cycling. S-parameter (blue, left axis) and applied current (amber bars, right axis) versus time for the CuTest4 experiment (67 DBS measure- ments, 50 keV, WSU). Three regimes are evident: initial cold-work baseline (S ∼0.409), thermal annealing under moderate current (S →0.396), and reversible current-induced defect creation above ∼30 A (S →0.42) with recovery below ∼20 A, repeated four times. Dashed line: annealed baseline. Error bars: ±1σ from counting statistics.

Figure 2 shows the DBS S-parameter and applied current versus time for the full CuTest4 experiment (67 measurements at 50 keV with a mean interval between mea- surements, or cadence, of ∼5.5 min), together forming the central observation of this work. Three successive regimes are resolved: an initial cold-work baseline at 0 A; a thermal-annealing regime as the current ramps through 0.2–30 A; and a reversible switching regime at currents above ∼30 A. During the initial 0 A interval (0–40 min), the as-received foil sits at S = 0.409 ± 0.001 (S/Sref = 1.040, N = 8), consistent with residual cold-work defects. Fig. 3 Threshold corroboration and depth profiles. a, Four-point resistivity versus current during a 0–53 A ramp. Measured resistivity (blue) is compared with two CRC thermal predictions bracketing the plausible surface emissivity of the foil (ε = 0.05 and 0.10). The thermal model predicts melting at 48–50 A; the foil remains intact up to 52 A. b, S-parameter versus positron beam energy for three specimens: the as-received electrolytic Cu foil (blue), the same foil after the complete CuTest4/5 current-cycling run (red; referred to in the text as the post-flash foil), and the Cu(100) single-crystal reference (grey). In the bulk (E > 25 keV), the post-flash foil and the single crystal agree...

Fig. 4 Nucleation and recombination kinetics. a, Boltzmann step fit to the final 13→38 A nucleation transient (CuTest4) of the form S(t) = Slow +∆S/(1+exp(−(t−t0)/w)). Rise ∆S/Slow = 6.64 ± 0.52%; Boltzmann width w = 2.42 ± 0.79 min. Note: w is the Boltzmann-sigmoidal width and is distinct from the exponential nucleation time constant τnuc reported in the main text; the two are different functional-form parameters for the same rising transient. b, Constrained exponential decay fit to CuTest5 (38→0 A) with recombination time constant τrec = 1.49 ± 0.37 min. The 1σ envelope (shaded) reflects the uncertainty on τrec. Defect recovery is faster than the measurement cadence. Error bars: ±1σ.

Fig. 5 DBS and PALS confirmation. a, WSU CuTest4 S-parameter versus physical state: “Pre (0 A)” is the initial cold-worked baseline, “8 A”–“38 A” are the mean S values at each step of the CuTest4 ramp, and “Post (0 A)” is the post-flash foil after the full CuTest4+CuTest5 cycle (single- crystal quality). NC State DBS results for the four analogous states (RT, 13 A, 38 A, post-flash) are plotted as orange diamonds against a facility-matched right-hand S axis. The left and right scales differ by a facility-specific additive constant because roughly half of the NC State annihilation signal comes from the silicon substrate carrying the 22Na source, which raises the apparent absolute S.
